runs.interpretation: Primary-earner substitution-response elasticity for earnings decile 1 in a U.S. microsimulation model
citations: Blundell, Duncan & Meghir (1998) - labor supply elasticities vary substantially across the earnings distribution, with lower earners often facing program interactions that complicate standard estimates
CBO (2012) - Labor Supply and Fiscal Implications of the ACA - uses compensated wage elasticities near 0.1–0.3 for low-income workers
Keane (2011) - Labor Supply and Taxes: A Survey - reviews compensated elasticities, noting higher values for some low-income groups due to program phase-outs
runs.reasoningSummary: Primary earners in decile 1 face complex incentive structures: very low base wages, potential interaction with means-tested benefits (EITC phase-in/phase-out, Medicaid), and possibly part-time or informal work. Substitution elasticities in microsimulation models for this group are typically set in the range of 0.1–0.3. They can be somewhat elevated relative to middle-income earners because of benefit cliffs and EITC incentive effects, but they can also be near zero if extensive-margin responses dominate and the model focuses on the intensive margin only. Standard U.S. microsimulation practice (e.g., Tax Policy Center, Penn Wharton, CBO) often applies compensated elasticities around 0.1–0.2 for low-income primary earners. Uncertainty is moderate because: (a) decile 1 is heterogeneous (some non-workers, some very low earners), (b) program interactions vary, and (c) the precise parameterization differs across models.

Substitution elasticities in microsimulation models for this group are typically set in the range of 0.1\u20130.3. They can be somewhat elevated relative to middle-income earners because of benefit cliffs and EITC incentive effects, but they can also be near zero if extensive-margin responses dominate and the model focuses on the intensive margin only. Standard U.S. microsimulation practice (e.g., Tax Policy Center, Penn Wharton, CBO) often applies compensated elasticities around 0.1\u20130.2 for low-income primary earners. Uncertainty is moderate because: (a) decile 1 is heterogeneous (some non-workers, some very low earners), (b) program interactions vary, and (c) the precise parameterization differs across models."}

runs.interpretation: Primary-earner substitution-response elasticity for earnings decile 1 in a U.S. microsimulation model
citations: Keane (2011) 'Labor Supply and Taxes: A Survey', Journal of Economic Literature — reviews compensated wage elasticities by income group, generally finding modest elasticities for primary earners.
CBO (2012) 'Effective Marginal Tax Rates for Low- and Moderate-Income Workers' — discusses labor supply incentive effects in lower earnings deciles under U.S. tax-benefit programs.
Eissa & Liebman (1996) 'Labor Supply Response to the Earned Income Tax Credit', QJE — documents labor supply responses for low-income primary earners around EITC policy changes.
runs.reasoningSummary: Primary earners in decile 1 face complex tax-benefit interactions (EITC phase-in, Medicaid, SNAP) that can create very low or even negative effective marginal wages, making substitution effects hard to isolate. The literature generally finds that primary earners have lower compensated wage elasticities than secondary earners. For low-income primary earners, substitution elasticities in U.S. microsimulation contexts are typically calibrated in the range of 0.10–0.25, reflecting modest but non-trivial responsiveness. There is uncertainty because (a) some models use near-zero values for primary earners regardless of decile, and (b) the EITC phase-in region can generate unusual incentive structures. I center my estimate at 0.15, with meaningful spread reflecting model-to-model variation.

runs.interpretation: Primary-earner substitution-response elasticity for earnings decile 1 in a U.S. microsimulation model
citations: Chetty (2012, Annual Review of Economics) — meta-analysis of labor supply elasticities, noting compensated elasticities around 0.1–0.3 for low-income primary earners
Congressional Budget Office (2012) — 'Labor Supply and Fiscal Policy' uses compensated elasticity of ~0.15 for primary earners
Blundell & MaCurdy (1999, Handbook of Labor Economics) — survey noting small compensated elasticities for primary (male) earners, typically 0.0–0.2
runs.reasoningSummary: Primary earners in decile 1 (lowest earnings) face complex tax-benefit interactions (EITC phase-in, Medicaid, SNAP) that can create high effective marginal tax rates, but labor supply literature consistently finds small compensated (substitution) elasticities for primary earners relative to secondary earners. Low-income primary earners may show slightly larger substitution responses than middle-income primary earners due to benefit phase-outs, but the standard calibration in U.S. microsimulation models (e.g., Tax Policy Center, CBO) places primary-earner compensated elasticities in the 0.1–0.2 range. I center at 0.15 with meaningful uncertainty spanning 0.0–0.45 at the extremes, reflecting both model-to-model variation and the possibility that some microsimulations use higher values for decile 1 to capture EITC incentive effects.

Low-income primary earners may show slightly larger substitution responses than middle-income primary earners due to benefit phase-outs, but the standard calibration in U.S. microsimulation models (e.g., Tax Policy Center, CBO) places primary-earner compensated elasticities in the 0.1\u20130.2 range. I center at 0.15 with meaningful uncertainty spanning 0.0\u20130.45 at the extremes, reflecting both model-to-model variation and the possibility that some microsimulations use higher values for decile 1 to capture EITC incentive effects."}

runs.interpretation: Primary-earner substitution-response elasticity for earnings decile 1 in a U.S. microsimulation model
citations: Chetty (2012, Annual Review of Economics) — meta-analysis of labor supply elasticities; compensated elasticity central estimates around 0.33 overall, lower for primary earners.
CBO (2012) — 'Labor Supply and Fiscal Policy'; uses compensated wage elasticities around 0.15–0.25 for primary earners.
Bargain et al. (2014, Scandinavian Journal of Economics) — cross-country microsimulation elasticities; primary earners in lower deciles tend to have modest substitution elasticities.
runs.reasoningSummary: Primary earners in decile 1 (the lowest earnings decile) are a heterogeneous group that includes workers facing high effective marginal tax rates from phase-outs (e.g., EITC) but also workers with strong constraints on hours (e.g., fixed-hours jobs, disability). The literature generally finds that primary-earner compensated (substitution) elasticities are modest, often in the 0.1–0.3 range, with some studies finding even lower values for low-income primary earners due to hours constraints. However, EITC phase-in and phase-out regions can produce unusual effective wage changes, adding uncertainty. I center my belief around 0.15 for the substitution elasticity in decile 1, acknowledging that some microsimulation models use values as low as 0.05 and others as high as 0.4 for this group.

runs.interpretation: Primary-earner substitution-response elasticity for earnings decile 1 in a U.S. microsimulation model
citations: Chetty, R. (2012). Bounds on elasticities with optimization frictions: A synthesis of micro and macro evidence on labor supply. Econometrica, 80(3), 969–1018.
CBO (2012). Labor Supply and Taxes. Congressional Budget Office Working Paper.
Keane, M. P. (2011). Labor supply and taxes: A survey. Journal of Economic Literature, 49(4), 961–1075.
runs.reasoningSummary: Primary earners in decile 1 (lowest earnings) are often constrained by non-participation or part-time work, welfare program phase-outs (e.g., EITC phase-in), and limited hours flexibility. Empirical literature on compensated (substitution) elasticities for low-income primary earners in U.S. microsimulation contexts (e.g., Tax Policy Center, CBO-style models) typically assigns modest positive substitution elasticities around 0.1–0.2 for this group. The EITC phase-in creates positive incentives on the extensive margin, but standard tax-benefit microsimulations often parameterize the Hicksian (substitution) elasticity for low-decile primary earners in the 0.1–0.3 range. I center at 0.15 with meaningful uncertainty given variation across model choices and empirical sources.

runs.reasoningSummary: Primary earners (typically male household heads in the literature) in the lowest earnings decile face complex budget constraints: high effective marginal tax rates from phase-outs of benefits (EITC plateau/phase-out, SNAP, Medicaid) can compress or even reverse substitution responses, but at the same time low-wage primary earners may have somewhat higher compensated wage elasticities than middle earners due to fewer hours constraints. However, the EITC phase-in region (relevant for decile 1) creates an implicit subsidy that may dampen net substitution response estimation. I center my belief around 0.15, with meaningful uncertainty spanning roughly 0.0 to 0.45 at the 5th–95th percentile range.

citations: Chetty, R. (2012). Bounds on Elasticities with Optimization Frictions. Econometrica.

runs.reasoningSummary: Primary earners at the bottom of the earnings distribution (decile 1) face complex incentive structures: high effective marginal tax rates from benefit phase-outs (EITC phase-in/plateau, SNAP, Medicaid) can suppress labor supply response, but the EITC phase-in region actually subsidizes work, potentially raising the compensated wage elasticity. The literature on compensated (substitution) elasticities for primary earners generally clusters around 0.1–0.4, with lower-income workers sometimes showing modestly higher elasticities due to benefit interactions. However, participation constraints and optimization frictions at the bottom of the distribution temper large responses. I center my median at 0.20, reflecting a moderate substitution response consistent with microsimulation conventions (e.g., CBO-style models), with meaningful uncertainty spanning roughly 0.05 to 0.55.

runs.reasoningSummary: Primary earners in decile 1 (lowest earnings) face complex incentives under U.S. tax-benefit rules (EITC phase-in creates a wage subsidy, Medicaid, SNAP interactions). The compensated (substitution) elasticity for primary earners is generally estimated to be modest—lower than for secondary earners. For the bottom decile specifically, the EITC phase-in region implies a positive effective marginal wage change, but these workers also face program interactions and participation constraints. Mainstream estimates for male/primary-earner compensated elasticities cluster around 0.1–0.2. The bottom decile may show slightly higher elasticity than middle earners due to participation margins blending in, but microsimulation models (e.g., Tax Policy Center, TAXSIM-based) typically apply values in the 0.1–0.3 range for low-income primary earners. I center at 0.15 with meaningful uncertainty spanning near-zero to ~0.45.

runs.interpretation: Primary-earner substitution-response elasticity for earnings decile 1 in a U.S. microsimulation model
citations: Blundell & MaCurdy (1999) - survey of labor supply elasticities, noting higher elasticities at lower earnings

runs.reasoningSummary: Primary earners in decile 1 (lowest earnings) face complex tax-benefit interactions including EITC phase-in and phase-out regions, Medicaid cliffs, and means-tested benefit tapering, which can produce high effective marginal tax rates and thus distort labor supply substantially. However, empirical consensus on compensated (substitution) elasticities for primary earners is typically in the range of 0.1–0.3 in the general population, with some evidence of larger responses at lower income levels due to benefit interactions. Microsimulation models often calibrate primary-earner substitution elasticities in this range. The EITC phase-in region could push the effective wage change response higher, but primary earners (as opposed to secondary earners) tend to have lower labor supply elasticities. I center my belief around 0.15 with meaningful uncertainty, reflecting that some models use values near 0.1 and others near 0.3 for this population.

runs.reasoningSummary: Primary earners in the lowest earnings decile (decile 1) tend to face strong participation constraints, limited hours flexibility, and high effective marginal tax rates from phase-ins of programs like the EITC. The literature generally finds modest compensated (substitution) elasticities for primary earners, typically in the 0.1–0.3 range overall. For decile 1, hours flexibility is often low (many are constrained at zero or full-time jobs), and some microsimulation models assign relatively small substitution elasticities to this group (often around 0.1 or below), because these workers may be more constrained by demand-side factors or participation barriers rather than wage incentives alone. The bounded-response framing (as in Chetty 2012) further compresses estimates. My median estimate is 0.1 with considerable uncertainty spanning near-zero to about 0.4.

runs.reasoningSummary: Primary earners in decile 1 are very low earners. In standard U.S. tax-benefit microsimulations (e.g., CBO-style models), primary-earner substitution elasticities are typically modest (often 0.1–0.3 overall) and sometimes slightly elevated or uncertain for the lowest decile because these workers face complex effective marginal rates from phase-ins of EITC and other transfers. However, many models assign primary earners uniformly low substitution elasticities (~0.1–0.2) regardless of decile, as primary earners are treated as less elastic than secondary earners. I center my belief around 0.15 with meaningful uncertainty, since different modeling choices and empirical estimates vary and the lowest decile has sparse data and atypical work patterns.

runs.interpretation: Primary-earner substitution-response elasticity for earnings decile 1 in a U.S. microsimulation model
citations: Chetty, R. (2012). Bounds on Elasticities with Optimization Frictions. Econometrica.
CBO (2012). Labor Supply and Taxes: A Review. Congressional Budget Office.
Keane, M. (2011). Labor Supply and Taxes: A Survey. Journal of Economic Literature.
runs.reasoningSummary: Primary earners in the lowest earnings decile tend to have limited labor supply flexibility due to fixed hours, institutional constraints, and the nature of low-wage work. Standard microsimulation models (e.g., those used by CBO or Tax Policy Center) typically assign compensated wage elasticities for primary earners in the range of 0.1–0.3 overall, with lower values for low-income primary earners who face high effective marginal tax rates (due to phase-outs of EITC and other benefits) but also have less margin to adjust hours. The literature on primary-earner elasticities generally finds modest substitution elasticities (0.1–0.2) for this group; the bottom decile may be slightly lower due to constraints, but the EITC phase-in region can introduce some positive response. I center my estimate at 0.1, with meaningful uncertainty spanning near-zero to ~0.4.

- Definition: Response coefficient multiplying the bounded relative change in the effective marginal wage for primary earners in earnings decile 1 in a U.S. tax-benefit simulation.
- Target interpretation: Primary-earner substitution-response elasticity for earnings decile 1 in a U.S. microsimulation model
- Population/context: Primary earners in earnings decile 1 in the United States under a current-law tax-benefit simulation
